description: Adds up a range or list of numbers.

arguments.name: number1
arguments.description: The first number or range to include.

arguments.name: number2
arguments.description: An additional number or range to include.

examples.explanation: Adds every number in cells A1 through A10.

hrUses: Totaling hours worked or payroll amounts across a pay period.
Summarizing headcount or cost by department.
Building the numeric backbone of a compensation or budget sheet.
commonMistakes: Including text cells in the range (SUM ignores them silently, which can hide data-entry errors).
Forgetting to update the range when new rows are added below it.
whyUse: SUM is usually the very first formula people learn, because adding up a column or row of numbers by hand is slow and error-prone. Any time you're totaling hours, costs, or quantities, SUM does it instantly and updates automatically if the numbers change.
steps: Click the cell where you want the total to appear.
Type =SUM( and then select the range of cells you want to add.
Close the parenthesis and press Enter.
If you add a new row inside that range later, the total updates on its own.

description: Calculates the mean of a range of numbers, ignoring blank cells and text.

arguments.name: number1
arguments.description: The first number or range to include.

arguments.name: number2
arguments.description: An additional number or range to include.

examples.explanation: Returns the average of the values in B2:B20.

hrUses: Totaling hours worked or payroll amounts across a pay period.
Summarizing headcount or cost by department.
Building the numeric backbone of a compensation or budget sheet.
commonMistakes: Blank cells are ignored, but cells containing 0 are counted — this can skew results if you meant to exclude zeros.
Text values are ignored rather than causing an error, which can mask bad data.
whyUse: AVERAGE gives you a single representative number for a group of values. It automatically ignores blank cells, so partial data doesn't skew the result the way it would with manual math.
steps: Select the cell where the average should appear.
Type =AVERAGE( and select the range of numbers.
Close the parenthesis and press Enter.
Remember: blank cells are ignored, but a cell containing 0 still counts.

description: Counts how many cells in a range contain numbers.

arguments.name: value1
arguments.description: The first value to check.

arguments.name: value2
arguments.description: An additional value to check.

examples.explanation: Returns how many numeric entries are in that range.

hrUses: Totaling hours worked or payroll amounts across a pay period.
Summarizing headcount or cost by department.
Building the numeric backbone of a compensation or budget sheet.
commonMistakes: COUNT only counts numbers — use COUNTA for cells with any content (text included).
whyUse: Counts how many cells in a range contain numbers. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your value1: The first value to check.
Work out your value2 (optional): An additional value to check.
Put it together following the syntax: =COUNT(value1, [value2], ...)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Counts non-empty cells, whether they contain numbers, text, or dates.

arguments.name: value1
arguments.description: The first value to check.

arguments.name: value2
arguments.description: An additional value to check.

examples.explanation: Returns how many cells in that range are not blank.

hrUses: Totaling hours worked or payroll amounts across a pay period.
Summarizing headcount or cost by department.
Building the numeric backbone of a compensation or budget sheet.
commonMistakes: A cell containing an empty string ("") from a formula still counts as non-blank.
whyUse: Counts non-empty cells, whether they contain numbers, text, or dates. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your value1: The first value to check.
Work out your value2 (optional): An additional value to check.
Put it together following the syntax: =COUNTA(value1, [value2], ...)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Counts cells in a range that meet one condition.

arguments.name: range
arguments.description: The range of cells to evaluate.

arguments.name: criteria
arguments.description: The condition a cell must meet to be included.

examples.explanation: Counts how many cells in C2:C50 equal "West".

hrUses: Totaling hours worked or payroll amounts across a pay period.
Summarizing headcount or cost by department.
Building the numeric backbone of a compensation or budget sheet.
commonMistakes: Text criteria need quotes; numbers usually don't (=COUNTIF(A1:A10,10)).
Wildcards (* and ?) work in criteria, e.g. "*west*" for partial matches.
whyUse: COUNTIF answers questions like "how many of these meet a condition?" It's one condition only; for more than one, you'd move up to COUNTIFS.
steps: Identify the range you want to check, e.g. a column of department names.
Decide the condition — an exact match ("HR"), a comparison (">100"), or a wildcard pattern.
Write =COUNTIF(range, criteria) with your range and condition in place.
Press Enter — the result is a count, not a list, of how many cells matched.

description: Counts cells that meet multiple conditions across one or more ranges, all combined with AND logic.

arguments.name: range1
arguments.description: Additional argument.

arguments.name: criteria1
arguments.description: The first condition to apply.

arguments.name: range2
arguments.description: Additional argument.

arguments.name: criteria2
arguments.description: An additional condition to apply.

examples.explanation: Counts rows where region is West and value exceeds 100.
Counts how many active employees are in the Sales department.

hrUses: Counting active headcount by department or location.
Tracking how many employees are on leave in a given period.
Auditing how many records are missing a required field.
commonMistakes: All ranges must be the same size, or you'll get a #VALUE! error.
There's no built-in OR logic — for OR conditions you typically need multiple COUNTIFS added together.
whyUse: Like SUMIFS but for counting — useful for questions with more than one condition.
steps: Identify your first condition: a criteria_range and its criteria.
Add further criteria_range/criteria pairs for each extra condition you need.
Write it together: =COUNTIFS(criteria_range1, criteria1, criteria_range2, criteria2, ...).
Remember it's AND logic only — every condition must be true for a row to be counted.

description: Adds up values in a range that meet one condition.

arguments.name: range
arguments.description: The range of cells to evaluate.

arguments.name: criteria
arguments.description: The condition a cell must meet to be included.

arguments.name: sum_range
arguments.description: The actual cells to add up (if different from the range being checked).

examples.explanation: Sums column D where column C equals "West".

hrUses: Totaling hours worked or payroll amounts across a pay period.
Summarizing headcount or cost by department.
Building the numeric backbone of a compensation or budget sheet.
commonMistakes: Forgetting sum_range defaults to the criteria range itself if omitted — usually not what you want.
Mismatched range sizes between the criteria range and sum range.
whyUse: Adds up values in a range that meet one condition. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your range: The range of cells to evaluate.
Work out your criteria: The condition a cell must meet to be included.
Work out your sum_range (optional): The actual cells to add up (if different from the range being checked).
Put it together following the syntax: =SUMIF(range, criteria, [sum_range])
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Adds up values that meet multiple conditions. Note the sum_range comes first here, unlike SUMIF.

arguments.name: sum_range
arguments.description: The actual cells to add up (if different from the range being checked).

arguments.name: criteria_range1
arguments.description: The first range of cells to check against a condition.

arguments.name: criteria1
arguments.description: The first condition to apply.

arguments.name: criteria_range2
arguments.description: An additional range of cells to check against a condition.

arguments.name: criteria2
arguments.description: An additional condition to apply.

examples.explanation: Sums column E only where C is "West" and D is greater than 100.
Totals payroll for the HR department in January only.

hrUses: Totaling payroll cost by department, location, or pay period.
Summing overtime hours only for employees over a threshold.
Building a self-updating compensation summary as new rows are added.
commonMistakes: Mixing up argument order with SUMIF — SUMIFS puts sum_range first.
All ranges must match in size.
whyUse: SUMIFS is what you reach for once a single condition (SUMIF) isn't enough — for example, totaling sales for a specific region AND a specific month at the same time.
steps: Identify the sum_range — the actual numbers you want to total.
Identify your first condition: a criteria_range and the criteria it must match.
Add additional criteria_range/criteria pairs for each extra condition — all must be true (AND logic).
Write it together: =SUMIFS(sum_range, criteria_range1, criteria1, criteria_range2, criteria2, ...).
Double-check every range is the exact same size.

description: Checks whether a condition is TRUE or FALSE, and returns one value for TRUE and a different value for FALSE. One of the most-used functions in Excel — the basic building block of decision-making in a spreadsheet.

arguments.name: logical_test
arguments.description: The condition to test — must evaluate to TRUE or FALSE.

arguments.name: value_if_true
arguments.description: What to return if the condition is TRUE.

arguments.name: value_if_false
arguments.description: What to return if the condition is FALSE.

examples.explanation: If A2 is 35 or more, shows "Pass"; otherwise shows "Fail".
If B2 contains "Yes", shows "Approved"; otherwise shows "Pending".
A nested IF that tests multiple thresholds in order to assign a letter grade.

hrUses: Marking attendance as Present or Absent based on a check-in time.
Flagging bonus eligibility once a sales or performance target is hit.
Assigning a pay grade or letter rating from a numeric score.
Approving or rejecting leave requests based on remaining balance.
commonMistakes: Leaving out value_if_false returns FALSE rather than blank, which can look odd in a report.
Nesting too many IFs gets hard to read — IFS or lookup formulas are often cleaner for many conditions.
whyUse: IF is how you make a spreadsheet make a decision instead of just displaying data. Anywhere you'd say "if this, then that" out loud about your data, IF can likely do it automatically.
steps: Decide what you're testing — a cell being over/under a number, equal to specific text, or blank. This becomes your logical_test.
Decide what should show up when that test is TRUE. This is your value_if_true.
Decide what should show up when it's FALSE. This is your value_if_false.
Put it together: =IF(logical_test, value_if_true, value_if_false).
Test it with a value that should pass, and one that should fail, to confirm both sides work.

description: Checks multiple conditions in order and returns the value for the first one that's true — a cleaner alternative to nested IFs.

arguments.name: condition1
arguments.description: The first condition to test.

arguments.name: value1
arguments.description: The first value to check.

arguments.name: condition2
arguments.description: An additional condition to test.

arguments.name: value2
arguments.description: An additional value to check.

examples.explanation: Grades a score.

hrUses: Flagging attendance, eligibility, or approval status automatically.
Building pass/fail or pay-grade decisions into a sheet.
Powering conditional formatting rules for HR dashboards.
commonMistakes: If no condition matches and there's no catch-all TRUE case, it returns #N/A.
Only available in newer Excel versions (2016+ with Office 365, or Excel 2019+).
whyUse: Checks multiple conditions in order and returns the value for the first one that's true — a cleaner alternative to nested IFs. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your condition1: The first condition to test.
Work out your value1: The first value to check.
Work out your condition2 (optional): An additional condition to test.
Put it together following the syntax: =IFS(condition1, value1, [condition2, value2], ...)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Returns TRUE only if every condition supplied is true.

arguments.name: logical1
arguments.description: The first condition to check.

arguments.name: logical2
arguments.description: An additional condition to check.

examples.explanation: Checks whether A1 is between 0 and 100.

hrUses: Flagging attendance, eligibility, or approval status automatically.
Building pass/fail or pay-grade decisions into a sheet.
Powering conditional formatting rules for HR dashboards.
commonMistakes: AND alone just returns TRUE/FALSE — it's usually combined inside an IF to produce a useful result.
whyUse: Returns TRUE only if every condition supplied is true. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your logical1: The first condition to check.
Work out your logical2 (optional): An additional condition to check.
Put it together following the syntax: =AND(logical1, [logical2], ...)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Returns TRUE if at least one of the supplied conditions is true.

arguments.name: logical1
arguments.description: The first condition to check.

arguments.name: logical2
arguments.description: An additional condition to check.

examples.explanation: Checks if A1 is either region.

hrUses: Flagging attendance, eligibility, or approval status automatically.
Building pass/fail or pay-grade decisions into a sheet.
Powering conditional formatting rules for HR dashboards.
commonMistakes: Like AND, it's usually wrapped in an IF rather than used alone.
whyUse: Returns TRUE if at least one of the supplied conditions is true. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your logical1: The first condition to check.
Work out your logical2 (optional): An additional condition to check.
Put it together following the syntax: =OR(logical1, [logical2], ...)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Reverses TRUE to FALSE and vice versa.

arguments.name: logical
arguments.description: The condition to reverse.

examples.explanation: Returns TRUE if A1 is not empty.

hrUses: Flagging attendance, eligibility, or approval status automatically.
Building pass/fail or pay-grade decisions into a sheet.
Powering conditional formatting rules for HR dashboards.
commonMistakes: Easy to double-negate accidentally and confuse the logic — read it carefully when nesting.
whyUse: Reverses TRUE to FALSE and vice versa. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your logical: The condition to reverse.
Put it together following the syntax: =NOT(logical)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Searches for a value in the first column of a table and returns a value from a specified column in the same row.

arguments.name: lookup_value
arguments.description: The value you're searching for.

arguments.name: table_array
arguments.description: The full table range that contains the data.

arguments.name: col_index_num
arguments.description: Which column (counting from the left of the table) to pull the result from.

arguments.name: range_lookup
arguments.description: FALSE for an exact match, TRUE for approximate — set this explicitly to avoid surprises.

examples.explanation: Finds "A102" in column A and returns the value from column C in that same row.
Looks up the employee ID in B2 against a named table and returns their department.

hrUses: Pulling an employee's manager, department, or salary band from a master roster by ID.
Matching a form submission to an existing employee record.
Building a payroll sheet that auto-fills details once an ID is entered.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the fourth argument (FALSE for exact match) — Excel defaults to approximate match, which silently returns wrong results if data isn't sorted.
Can only look to the right of the lookup column — it can't return values from columns to the left.
whyUse: VLOOKUP solves a very common problem: you have two related tables, and you want to automatically pull matching information from one into the other, instead of manually searching and copying.
steps: Identify your lookup_value — the thing you're searching for.
Identify the table_array — the full range of the table you're searching in. The lookup value must be in that table's first column.
Count which column (from the left, starting at 1) holds the value you want returned — that's col_index_num.
Almost always set the last argument to FALSE for an exact match.
Write it together: =VLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, col_index_num, FALSE).

description: Like VLOOKUP but searches across the first row of a table instead of the first column.

arguments.name: lookup_value
arguments.description: The value you're searching for.

arguments.name: table_array
arguments.description: The full table range that contains the data.

arguments.name: row_index_num
arguments.description: Which row (counting from the top of the table) to pull the result from.

arguments.name: range_lookup
arguments.description: FALSE for an exact match, TRUE for approximate — set this explicitly to avoid surprises.

examples.explanation: Finds "Q3" in row 1 and returns the value from row 5 in that column.

hrUses: Pulling an employee's department, manager, or pay grade from a master roster.
Matching timesheet entries to the right employee record.
Building a self-updating org chart or lookup table.
commonMistakes: Same exact-match trap as VLOOKUP — always set the last argument to FALSE unless you specifically need approximate matching.
whyUse: Like VLOOKUP but searches across the first row of a table instead of the first column. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your lookup_value: The value you're searching for.
Work out your table_array: The full table range that contains the data.
Work out your row_index_num: Which row (counting from the top of the table) to pull the result from.
Put it together following the syntax: =HLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, row_index_num, [range_lookup])
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Returns the value at a given row/column position within a range — most powerful when paired with MATCH.

arguments.name: array
arguments.description: The range or array of values.

arguments.name: row_num
arguments.description: Additional argument.

arguments.name: col_num
arguments.description: Additional argument.

examples.explanation: Returns the 5th value in that range.
Combined with MATCH, looks up a salary by employee ID — a flexible alternative to VLOOKUP.

hrUses: Building lookups that can pull from a column to the left of the search column.
Powering flexible, two-way lookups (row and column) for large HR tables.
Replacing brittle VLOOKUP formulas that break when columns are inserted.
commonMistakes: Used alone it needs a fixed position number — pair with MATCH to find that position dynamically instead of hardcoding it.
whyUse: INDEX on its own just retrieves a value by position, but it becomes powerful paired with MATCH — together they replicate (and improve on) what VLOOKUP does.
steps: Identify the array — the range you want to pull a value from.
Determine the row_num (and col_num if needed) of the value you want.
For a static position, write =INDEX(array, row_num) directly.
For a dynamic position, nest a MATCH formula in place of row_num.

description: Returns the position of a value within a range, rather than the value itself.

arguments.name: lookup_value
arguments.description: The value you're searching for.

arguments.name: lookup_array
arguments.description: The range to search within.

arguments.name: match_type
arguments.description: 0 for an exact match, 1 or -1 for approximate — 0 is almost always what you want.

examples.explanation: Returns 3, since "March" is the third item in the range.
Finds the row position of employee ID "A102" within a list.

hrUses: Finding the row position of an employee record for use inside INDEX.
Checking whether a value exists in a list at all.
Powering dynamic dropdowns that need to know an item's position.
commonMistakes: match_type 0 (exact) is what most people want but isn't the default (1) — always specify it.
whyUse: MATCH doesn't return a value — it returns a position. That makes it the perfect partner for INDEX: MATCH finds where something is, and INDEX fetches what's there.
steps: Identify the lookup_value you're searching for.
Identify the lookup_array — the single range you're searching within.
Set match_type to 0 for an exact match.
Write it together: =MATCH(lookup_value, lookup_array, 0).

description: Modern replacement for VLOOKUP/HLOOKUP — searches any column and returns from any other column, in either direction, with exact match by default.

arguments.name: lookup_value
arguments.description: The value you're searching for.

arguments.name: lookup_array
arguments.description: The range to search within.

arguments.name: return_array
arguments.description: The range to pull matching results from.

arguments.name: if_not_found
arguments.description: What to show if nothing matches, instead of an error.

arguments.name: match_mode
arguments.description: Controls how matching is performed (exact, approximate, or wildcard).

arguments.name: search_mode
arguments.description: Controls the search direction (first-to-last or last-to-first).

examples.explanation: Looks up "A102" and returns the matching value from column C, or shows "Not found" if there's no match.
An exact-match lookup that searches from the bottom up, useful for finding the most recent record.

hrUses: Replacing older VLOOKUP-based HR templates with a more flexible, error-resistant lookup.
Pulling data from a column to the left of the lookup column, which VLOOKUP can't do.
Building lookups that show a custom message instead of an error.
commonMistakes: Only available in Excel 365 / Excel 2021+ — older versions need VLOOKUP or INDEX/MATCH instead.
whyUse: XLOOKUP does everything VLOOKUP does, but more flexibly — it can search in either direction, return from a column to the left of the search column, and show a custom message instead of an error when nothing matches.
steps: Identify the lookup_value you're searching for.
Identify the lookup_array — the single column you're searching in.
Identify the return_array — the column you want a value pulled from.
Optionally add a fourth argument for if_not_found.
Write it together: =XLOOKUP(lookup_value, lookup_array, return_array, "Not found").

description: Returns a fallback value if a formula produces an error, otherwise returns the formula's normal result.

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The value to evaluate.

arguments.name: value_if_error
arguments.description: Additional argument.

examples.explanation: ,"Not found") shows "Not found" instead of #N/A.

hrUses: Flagging attendance, eligibility, or approval status automatically.
Building pass/fail or pay-grade decisions into a sheet.
Powering conditional formatting rules for HR dashboards.
commonMistakes: Wrapping everything in IFERROR can hide real bugs you'd otherwise want to notice and fix.
whyUse: Formulas fail sometimes. IFERROR lets you show something readable instead of an error code, without changing what the formula does when it works correctly.
steps: Take your existing formula that sometimes errors.
Wrap it as the first argument: =IFERROR(your_formula, ...).
Add what should display instead of the error as the second argument.
The formula still works normally when there's no error.

description: Like IFERROR, but only catches #N/A errors specifically, letting other errors (like #DIV/0!) still show through.

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The value to evaluate.

arguments.name: value_if_na
arguments.description: Additional argument.

examples.explanation: ,"Not found") only replaces #N/A results.

hrUses: Flagging attendance, eligibility, or approval status automatically.
Building pass/fail or pay-grade decisions into a sheet.
Powering conditional formatting rules for HR dashboards.
commonMistakes: People often reach for IFERROR by habit when IFNA is actually the more precise choice for lookup formulas.
whyUse: Like IFERROR, but only catches #N/A errors specifically, letting other errors (like #DIV/0!) still show through. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your value: The value to evaluate.
Work out your value_if_na: Additional argument.
Put it together following the syntax: =IFNA(value, value_if_na)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Joins multiple text values into one. Largely superseded by CONCAT and TEXTJOIN.

arguments.name: text1
arguments.description: The first text value.

arguments.name: text2
arguments.description: Additional argument.

examples.explanation: Joins A1 and B1 with a space between them.

hrUses: Cleaning inconsistent name or ID formatting from HRIS exports.
Standardizing job titles or department names before reporting.
Merging first and last names or building email addresses from name parts.
commonMistakes: Microsoft recommends CONCAT or TEXTJOIN in newer versions — CONCATENATE is kept only for compatibility.
whyUse: Joins multiple text values into one. Largely superseded by CONCAT and TEXTJOIN. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your text1: The first text value.
Work out your text2 (optional): Additional argument.
Put it together following the syntax: =CONCATENATE(text1, [text2], ...)
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Joins multiple values with a chosen delimiter, and can skip blank cells automatically.

arguments.name: delimiter
arguments.description: The character(s) to place between joined values.

arguments.name: ignore_empty
arguments.description: TRUE to skip blank cells while joining.

arguments.name: text1
arguments.description: The first text value.

arguments.name: text2
arguments.description: Additional argument.

examples.explanation: Joins non-blank cells in A1:A5 separated by commas.

hrUses: Cleaning inconsistent name or ID formatting from HRIS exports.
Standardizing job titles or department names before reporting.
Merging first and last names or building email addresses from name parts.
commonMistakes: Requires Excel 2019+/365 — not available in older versions.
whyUse: TEXTJOIN is the modern way to combine text from multiple cells with a separator between them, and it can automatically skip blanks.
steps: Decide your delimiter — the character(s) between each joined value.
Decide whether to skip blank cells (usually TRUE).
List the cells or range to join.
Write it together: =TEXTJOIN(", ", TRUE, A2:A5).

description: Returns a specified number of characters from the start of a text string.

arguments.name: text
arguments.description: The text string to work with.

arguments.name: num_chars
arguments.description: How many characters to return.

examples.explanation: Returns the first 3 characters of A1.

hrUses: Cleaning inconsistent name or ID formatting from HRIS exports.
Standardizing job titles or department names before reporting.
Merging first and last names or building email addresses from name parts.
commonMistakes: Counts characters, not words — LEFT("New York",3) returns "New", not the first word necessarily if it's shorter/longer than expected.
whyUse: Returns a specified number of characters from the start of a text string. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your text: The text string to work with.
Work out your num_chars (optional): How many characters to return.
Put it together following the syntax: =LEFT(text, [num_chars])
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Returns a specified number of characters from the end of a text string.

arguments.name: text
arguments.description: The text string to work with.

arguments.name: num_chars
arguments.description: How many characters to return.

examples.explanation: Returns the last 4 characters of A1.

hrUses: Cleaning inconsistent name or ID formatting from HRIS exports.
Standardizing job titles or department names before reporting.
Merging first and last names or building email addresses from name parts.
commonMistakes: If the string is shorter than num_chars, it just returns the whole string rather than erroring.
whyUse: Returns a specified number of characters from the end of a text string. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your text: The text string to work with.
Work out your num_chars (optional): How many characters to return.
Put it together following the syntax: =RIGHT(text, [num_chars])
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

description: Extracts a substring from the middle of a text string, starting at a given position.

arguments.name: text
arguments.description: The text string to work with.

arguments.name: start_num
arguments.description: The character position to start from (counting from 1).

arguments.name: num_chars
arguments.description: How many characters to return.

examples.explanation: Returns 2 characters starting at position 4.

hrUses: Cleaning inconsistent name or ID formatting from HRIS exports.
Standardizing job titles or department names before reporting.
Merging first and last names or building email addresses from name parts.
commonMistakes: start_num must be 1 or greater — 0 causes an error.
whyUse: Extracts a substring from the middle of a text string, starting at a given position. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your text: The text string to work with.
Work out your start_num: The character position to start from (counting from 1).
Work out your num_chars: How many characters to return.
Put it together following the syntax: =MID(text, start_num, num_chars)

Flagging upcoming work anniversaries or contract renewals.
Counting working days for leave or payroll calculations.

whyUse: Builds a proper date value from separate year, month, and day numbers. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.

description: Calculates the difference between two dates in days, months, or years, depending on the unit code ("d", "m", or "y").

arguments.name: start_date
arguments.description: The earlier date in the calculation.

arguments.name: end_date
arguments.description: The later date in the calculation.

arguments.name: unit
arguments.description: "d" for days, "m" for months, or "y" for years.

examples.explanation: Returns the number of full years between two dates.

hrUses: Calculating tenure, age, or time until a review date.
Flagging upcoming work anniversaries or contract renewals.
Counting working days for leave or payroll calculations.
commonMistakes: It's an undocumented function in Excel's formula help menu, though it works fine and is widely used — just don't expect autocomplete tooltips.
whyUse: DATEDIF answers "how much time is between these two dates" in whatever unit you need, making it the standard way to calculate age, tenure, or time remaining.
steps: Identify your start_date and end_date — both need to be real date values.
Choose your unit: "d" for days, "m" for full months, or "y" for full years.
Write it together: =DATEDIF(start_date, end_date, "y").
It won't appear in Excel's formula autocomplete — that's normal, it still works.

Summarizing headcount or cost by department.

description: Returns a list of unique values from a range, spilling the results into adjacent cells automatically.

arguments.name: array
arguments.description: The range or array of values.

arguments.name: by_col
arguments.description: TRUE to operate across columns instead of rows.

arguments.name: exactly_once
arguments.description: Additional argument.

examples.explanation: Lists each distinct value in that range once.

hrUses: Pulling an employee's department, manager, or pay grade from a master roster.
Matching timesheet entries to the right employee record.
Building a self-updating org chart or lookup table.
commonMistakes: Requires Excel 365/2021+ — spill formulas aren't available in older versions.
If the spill range isn't empty, you'll get a #SPILL! error.
whyUse: Returns a list of unique values from a range, spilling the results into adjacent cells automatically. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.

Work out your array: The range or array of values.
Work out your by_col (optional): TRUE to operate across columns instead of rows.
Work out your exactly_once (optional): Additional argument.

Building a self-updating org chart or lookup table.

whyUse: Sorts a range or array and spills the sorted result into the sheet, without altering the original data. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your array: The range or array of values.

description: Calculates the periodic payment for a loan based on constant payments and a constant interest rate.

arguments.name: rate
arguments.description: The interest rate for one period.

arguments.name: nper
arguments.description: The total number of payment periods.

arguments.name: pv
arguments.description: The present value - what a series of payments is worth right now.

arguments.name: fv
arguments.description: The future value you want to end up with (optional, defaults to 0).

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: When payments are due: 0 for end of period (default), 1 for start.

examples.explanation: Calculates the monthly payment on a $20,000 loan at 5% annual interest over 60 months.

hrUses: Modeling loan or benefit payment schedules.
Calculating amortization for relocation or tuition-assistance loans.
Projecting the future value of retirement contributions.
commonMistakes: The interest rate must match the payment period — divide an annual rate by 12 for monthly payments.
pv is usually entered as negative (money going out) to get a positive payment result.
whyUse: Calculates the periodic payment for a loan based on constant payments and a constant interest rate. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your rate: The interest rate for one period.
Work out your nper: The total number of payment periods.
Work out your pv: The present value - what a series of payments is worth right now.
Put it together following the syntax: =PMT(rate, nper, pv, [fv], [type])
Enter the formula and confirm the result looks right, then test with a different input.

arguments.description: The interest rate for one period.

arguments.name: nper
arguments.description: The total number of payment periods.

arguments.name: pmt
arguments.description: The fixed payment made each period.

arguments.description: The present value - what a series of payments is worth right now.

arguments.name: type

Calculating amortization for relocation or tuition-assistance loans.
Projecting the future value of retirement contributions.
commonMistakes: Same rate/period matching issue as PMT — mismatched periods give wildly wrong results.
whyUse: Calculates the future value of an investment based on periodic, constant payments and a constant interest rate. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.
Work out your rate: The interest rate for one period.
Work out your nper: The total number of payment periods.

Projecting the future value of retirement contributions.

whyUse: Calculates the present value of a future stream of payments, given a constant interest rate. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.

steps: Identify the values or ranges you need for each argument.

Merging first and last names or building email addresses from name parts.

whyUse: Converts a number or date into text, displayed in a specific format. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.

Building pass/fail or pay-grade decisions into a sheet.
Powering conditional formatting rules for HR dashboards.

whyUse: Returns TRUE if a value or formula results in any error type. It's especially useful any time you'd otherwise do this calculation by hand, since the result updates automatically whenever your underlying data changes.
